eli3: A sample space Ω is the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. Flipping a coin: Ω = {heads, tails}. Rolling a die: Ω = {1,2,3,4,5,6}. It's the universe of everything that could happen.

Events are subsets of the sample space — collections of outcomes we're interested in. 'Rolling an even number' = {2,4,6}. The probability of an event is its 'size' relative to the whole sample space (for equally likely outcomes).

Events combine with set operations: union (A or B), intersection (A and B), complement (not A). These correspond to 'or', 'and', 'not' in probability statements. Understanding these operations is crucial for computing complex probabilities.

whenWhy.title: Modeling random experiments
whenWhy.body: Any probabilistic scenario starts with defining Ω. A/B testing: Ω = all possible user responses. Queueing theory: Ω = all possible arrival/departure patterns. Clear sample space definition prevents confusion in probability calculations.

whenWhy.title: Event algebra and probability rules
whenWhy.body: Computing P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B) requires understanding events as sets. Independence means P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B). Mutual exclusivity means A ∩ B = ∅.

whenWhy.title: Sigma-algebras and measure theory
whenWhy.body: For rigorous probability, we define events as a sigma-algebra F: closed under complements and countable unions. This ensures we can assign probabilities consistently. Needed for continuous sample spaces where not all subsets can be measured.

whenWhy.title: Conditional probability foundations
whenWhy.body: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) requires defining the event A ∩ B. Bayes' theorem manipulates these set relationships. All of Bayesian inference builds on these basic set operations.

deepDiveIntro: A probability space is a triple (Ω, F, P) where Ω is the sample space (outcomes), F is a sigma-algebra of events (measurable subsets), and P: F → [0,1] is a probability measure satisfying P(Ω) = 1 and countable additivity for disjoint events.

For finite Ω, F is typically the power set (all subsets). For continuous Ω (e.g., ℝ), F is the Borel sigma-algebra (smallest sigma-algebra containing all intervals). Events follow set laws: De Morgan's laws, distributive laws, etc. These underpin all probability calculations.

deepDive.heading: Sample Space Types
deepDive.body: Sample spaces can be discrete, continuous, or a mixture of both. That distinction matters because it determines how probabilities are assigned and what mathematical tools are appropriate. Counting arguments work naturally in discrete spaces, while continuous spaces need measures and densities.
deepDive.formula: Discrete: Ω = {ω₁, ω₂, …}, Continuous: Ω ⊆ ℝⁿ

deepDive.heading: Events as Sets
deepDive.body: An event is simply a set of outcomes you care about. Because events are sets, you can combine them with the usual set operations to represent logical ideas such as or, and, and not. This is the language that lets probability problems be translated into clean algebra on sets.
deepDive.formula: A ∪ B, A ∩ B, Aᶜ, P(Aᶜ) = 1 - P(A)

deepDive.heading: Sigma-Algebra
deepDive.body: A sigma-algebra specifies which subsets are allowed to count as measurable events. It is designed so that complements and countable combinations of measurable events remain measurable, which is exactly what probability calculations need. This becomes essential in continuous spaces, where not every imaginable subset can be given a sensible probability.
deepDive.formula: Aᵢ ∈ F ⟹ ∪ᵢ Aᵢ ∈ F, A ∈ F ⟹ Aᶜ ∈ F

deepDive.heading: Probability Measure Properties
deepDive.body: A probability measure assigns consistent numerical weights to events. The basic rules say the whole sample space has probability one and that disjoint events add in the expected way. From those axioms you get familiar rules for complements, unions, and monotonicity.
deepDive.formula: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B)

deepDive.heading: Partitions and Law of Total Probability
deepDive.body: A partition breaks the sample space into non-overlapping pieces that cover every possible outcome. Once you do that, any event probability can be decomposed into contributions from each piece. This is the bookkeeping idea behind conditioning, mixture models, and Bayes' rule.

takeaways: Sample space Ω = all possible outcomes of random experiment.
Events are subsets of Ω; combine via union, intersection, complement.
Probability measure P satisfies P(Ω)=1 and countable additivity.
Sigma-algebra F defines which subsets are measurable events.
Partitions allow law of total probability: P(A) = Σ P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ).

edgeCases.q: What is the probability of the empty event?
edgeCases.a: P(∅) = 0 always. But P(A) = 0 doesn't mean A = ∅. In continuous probability (e.g., uniform on [0,1]), P(X = 0.5) = 0 but the event {0.5} is not empty. This is why probability density functions are needed.

Not in continuous spaces. The Banach-Tarski paradox shows we can partition a sphere into non-measurable pieces using the axiom of choice. We restrict to sigma-algebras (like Borel sets) where consistent probability measures exist.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between outcomes and events?
edgeCases.a: Outcomes are elements ω ∈ Ω (atomic). Events are subsets A ⊆ Ω (collections of outcomes). For finite Ω, single outcomes {ω} are events. In continuous spaces, individual outcomes have probability 0 but aren't technically events in the sigma-algebra sense.

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 1
Feller — An Introduction to Probability Theory, Vol. 1
